perturbation.original_text: At any time after termination of discussions by either Party with respect to the Transaction, upon the request of the Furnishing Party, the Receiving Party shall promptly (and in no event later than five business days after such request) (a) redeliver or cause to be redelivered to the Furnishing Party all copies of all Primary Evaluation Material in the possession or control of the Receiving Party or its Representatives which is in a visual or written format and erase or destroy all copies of all such Primary Evaluation Material which is stored in electronic format, and (b) destroy or cause to be destroyed all Derivative Evaluation Material in the possession or control of the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: At any time after termination of discussions by either Party with respect to the Transaction, upon the request of the Furnishing Party, the Receiving Party shall promptly (and in no event later than thirty business days after such request) (a) redeliver or cause to be redelivered to the Furnishing Party all copies of all Primary Evaluation Material in the possession or control of the Receiving Party or its Representatives which is in a visual or written format and erase or destroy all copies of all such Primary Evaluation Material which is stored in electronic format, and (b) destroy or cause to be destroyed all Derivative Evaluation Material in the possession or control of the Receiving Party or any of its Representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the deadline for returning confidential information from 5 to 30 days might conflict with the Georgia Trade Secrets Act, which requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Georgia Trade Secrets Act requires a trade secret owner to take reasonable steps to maintain its secrecy. Delaying the return of confidential information to 30 days might not be considered a reasonable step, especially if the information is highly sensitive and could be easily misused. This could potentially jeopardize trade secret protection under Georgia law.

perturbation.justification: The modification, which extends the deadline for returning confidential information from 5 to 30 days, creates an inconsistency with the Georgia Trade Secrets Act. The law explanation states that delaying the return of confidential information may not be considered a 'reasonable step' to maintain secrecy, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection. The provided perturbation definition states that inconsistencies arise when time-sensitive obligations in a contract do not align with legal requirements. Since the change modifies a time-sensitive obligation that arguably conflicts with maintaining the secrecy of a trade secret, this falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction', making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of one year subsequent to the termination of discussions regarding a Transaction, neither Party shall, without the prior written consent of the other Party, directly or indirectly solicit for hire any person currently employed by the other Party (or any of its subsidiaries) with whom the hiring Party first has contact, or who first becomes known to the hiring Party.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of five years subsequent to the termination of discussions regarding a Transaction, neither Party shall, without the prior written consent of the other Party, directly or indirectly solicit for hire any person currently employed by the other Party (or any of its subsidiaries) with whom the hiring Party first has contact, or who first becomes known to the hiring Party.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to five years may be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade under Georgia law, particularly if it unduly restricts an individual's ability to find employment.
perturbation.law_explanation: Georgia law scrutinizes non-compete and non-solicitation agreements to ensure they are reasonable in time, territory, and scope. A five-year non-solicitation clause is more likely to be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable, especially if it places an undue burden on former employees or significantly limits their employment opportunities.

perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the non-solicitation period from one year to five years. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this may be an unreasonable restraint of trade under Georgia law, and the Law Explanation supports that Georgia law scrutinizes non-compete and non-solicitation agreements for reasonableness in time. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the consensus of the explanations is that a five-year restriction is likely to be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable. This creates a legal contradiction because the modified contract term violates a legal standard for enforceability under Georgia law.

perturbation.original_text: For a period one year from the date of this Agreement, neither Party shall, without the prior written consent of the other Party or the other Party's board of directors, either directly or indirectly through any affiliate or Representative or otherwise: (i) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, by purchase, tender offer, merger, consolidation, share exchange or otherwise, ownership or control of any voting securities, or any direct or indirect right to acquire any voting securities, of the other Party, any subsidiary thereof, or any successor corporation thereto; (ii) make, or in any way participate in any 'solicitation' of 'proxies' (as such terms are used in the rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission) to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of, any voting securities of the other Party; (iii) seek or propose to influence or control the management or policies of the other Party; (iv) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit a proposal for, or offer of any merger, acquisition or other business combination or extraordinary transaction involving the other Party or any of its subsidiaries or any securities or assets of the other Party or any of its subsidiaries; (v) form, join or in any way participate in a 'group'( as defined in Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended) in connection with any of the foregoing, or (vi) request the other Party or any of the other party's Representatives to amend or waive any provision of this paragraph 5(b) in any manner which may reasonably be expected to compel or result in public disclosure; provided that such limitations shall expire upon the public announcement of a third party tender offer for more than fifty percent of the outstanding voting 3 securities of the other Party or a business combination between the other Party and a third party.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of ten years from the date of this Agreement, neither Party shall, without the prior written consent of the other Party or the other Party's board of directors, either directly or indirectly through any affiliate or Representative or otherwise: (i) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, by purchase, tender offer, merger, consolidation, share exchange or otherwise, ownership or control of any voting securities, or any direct or indirect right to acquire any voting securities, of the other Party, any subsidiary thereof, or any successor corporation thereto; (ii) make, or in any way participate in any 'solicitation' of 'proxies' (as such terms are used in the rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission) to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of, any voting securities of the other Party; (iii) seek or propose to influence or control the management or policies of the other Party; (iv) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit a proposal for, or offer of any merger, acquisition or other business combination or extraordinary transaction involving the other Party or any of its subsidiaries or any securities or assets of the other Party or any of its subsidiaries; (v) form, join or in any way participate in a 'group'( as defined in Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended) in connection with any of the foregoing, or (vi) request the other Party or any of the other party's Representatives to amend or waive any provision of this paragraph 5(b) in any manner which may reasonably be expected to compel or result in public disclosure; provided that such limitations shall expire upon the public announcement of a third party tender offer for more than fifty percent of the outstanding voting 3 securities of the other Party or a business combination between the other Party and a third party.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the standstill provision to ten years can be considered an unreasonable restraint on trade and potentially violate SEC regulations regarding tender offers and shareholder rights if it unduly restricts corporate governance and market activity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Williams Act (Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Williams Act aims to provide shareholders with adequate information and time to make informed decisions regarding tender offers. A ten-year standstill agreement could be viewed as an unreasonable barrier that prevents potential acquirers from making offers, effectively entrenching management and limiting shareholder options, thus conflicting with the intent of the Williams Act.

CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 , as amended, 29 U.S.C. 1001 et seq. (“ ERISA ”) that is subject to the provisions of ERISA , or any such plan that is not subject to ERISA that is maintained primarily for the benefit of the employees of a state or local government or instrumentality, or an endowment fund; (G) A parent holding company or control person, provided the aggregate amount held directly by the parent or control person, and directly and indirectly by their subsidiaries or affiliates that are not persons specified in § 240.13d-1(b)(1)(ii)(A) through (J), does not exceed one percent of the securities of the subject class; (H) A savings association as defined in Section 3(b) of the Federal Deposit Insurance Act ( 12 U.S.C. 1813 ); (I) A church plan that is excluded from the definition of an investment company under section 3(c)(14) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-3 ); (J) A non-U.S. institution that is the functional equivalent of any of the institutions listed in § 240.13d-1 (b)(1)(ii)(A) through (I), so long as the non-U.S. institution is subject to a regulatory scheme that is substantially comparable to the regulatory scheme applicable to the equivalent U.S. institution; and (K) A group, provided that all the members are persons specified in § 240.13d-1(b)(1)(ii)(A) through (J). (iii) Such person has promptly notified any other person (or group within the meaning of section 13(d)(3) of the Act) on whose beha
perturbation.justification: The change extends the standstill provision from one year to ten years. According to the Law Explanation, the Williams Act intends to provide shareholders with information and time when it comes to tender offers. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation argue a ten-year standstill agreement could be an unreasonable barrier that prevents potential acquirers from making offers, limiting shareholder options, and entrenching management. The original contract limits these actions for one year, while the modified contract limits these actions for ten years. This makes it harder for shareholders to make informed decisions and provides an unreasonable barrier for potential acquirers, thus creating a contradiction under the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that the Evaluation Material may contain material nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party. Each Party further acknowledges its awareness of the restrictions imposed by federal and state securities laws 2 on persons in possession of material nonpublic information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that the Evaluation Material may contain material nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party. Each Party explicitly agrees that trading on any such material nonpublic information is permissible under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that trading on material nonpublic information is permissible directly contradicts federal securities laws prohibiting insider trading.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security, including trading on the basis of material nonpublic information. This modification explicitly permits conduct that is illegal under federal law.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts federal securities laws by stating that trading on material nonpublic information is permissible, whereas the original text acknowledges restrictions imposed by these laws. The provided snippet from 15 USC 78j states that it is unlawful to use any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. Allowing insider trading directly violates this statute as it constitutes a deceptive device.
